title: Australia could be rationing fuel within weeks, says Rabobank strategist | The Business | ABC NEWS
description: The Australian share market slumped to a ten-month low, weighed down by miners, real estate and industrials.  The sell-off was widespread and even sharper on the other Asian markets, as traders worried about the escalating threats by Washington and Tehran to target energy infrastructure as the war enters its fourth week. Senior macro strategist with Rabobank, Ben Picton says volatility will likely continue for several days. In the longer term, he says some of the panic buying and supply chain shock is similar to what we experienced during COVID. The difference is, this time round, we're seeing these disruptions with an economy that already has an inflation problem. "So we're seeing interest rates going up, whereas during COVID, we were cutting interest rates," he says.  "If the supply side shock turns into a big drop off in demand and we start seriously throwing around the R-word, recession, then maybe the conversation changes and we might be talking about rate cuts at some point in the future." And in the face of rumoured stoppages to bunker fuel exports from Singapore, he says it's possible Australian supplies could be rationed within weeks. #ABCBusiness Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: Gold and silver fall as war in Iran continues to wreak havoc on financial markets | Finance Report
description: The ASX 200 recouped much of its earlier losses to close 0.7 per cent lower, down to a level not seen in 10 months.  The miners, and in particular gold miners, were sold off, along with the major banks as fears mounted about a slowing global economy. Brent crude oil rose again, trading at above $US113 a barrel, with the US and Iran exchanging threats related to the Strait of Hormuz. title: Builders warn of price hikes as Middle East war upends supply chains | The Business | ABC NEWS
description: Alarm bells are ringing in Australia's residential building industry, with some describing "considerable" supply chain disruption and price surges on crucial items as being "like COVID" again. Suppliers are bringing in "emergency fuel levies" for everything from sand to concrete as petrol prices soar due to the war in the Middle East. The chaos could ripple even further, with the industry warning about rising freight costs on materials that need to be imported. Major companies that supply pipes for plumbing are even warning of shortages.  There are fears the situation will make building a home even more expensive in 2026, further threatening the Australian government's pledge to build 1.2 million homes over five years.    Master Builders' Denita Wawn shared similar concerns about the federal government's housing targets, and what the latest supply chain shocks would do to the price of the Australian dream.  The federal Housing Minister Clare O'Neil described the target as "aspirational".  Rising building costs will add to inflation. The Reserve Bank of Australia has already been hiking interest rates to tackle resurging inflation in 2026, which will also add to the costs for everyday Australians borrowing to build a home. #ABCBusiness  Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE Read more here: https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-23/building-supply-chain-impact-and-shortages/106468234  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: Katherine spared from major flooding as homes elsewhere are inundated and without power | ABC NEWS
description: 00:00 While the Northern Territory town of Katherine has escaped the full wrath of ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle, authorities are shifting focus towards a smaller nearby community. Adelaide River is one of four NT waterways under emergency warnings after recording hundreds of millimeters of rain which has left 200 residents without power. By Courtney Barrett Peters for ABC News Northern Territory.  02:08 Reporter Olivana Lathouris gives an update on how the community of Katherine is feeling in the wake of Ex Tropical Cyclone Narelle, and the clean-up efforts going forward. title: Indian Rupee hits record low, experts predict it will fall further | The World | ABC News
description: The Indian Rupee has hit record lows today against the US dollar and other major global currencies. There has been a signifciant downward trend for several years now. At this time last year (March 23, 2025), one US dollar was worth 86 Rupees - today it's fetching nearly 94 Rupees - that's a nearly 9 per cent drop.
